name: templates-settings-app
description: New grammar under a fresh agent: repeated grouped toggle sections where <template>/<use> is the natural shape, plus token style attributes.
prompt:
This workspace is a freshly scaffolded Native SDK native-frontend app (see README.md; the view is src/app.native, the logic src/main.zig). Replace the counter with a small Settings app.

Requirements:
- Three settings sections named exactly "General", "Notifications", and "Privacy". Each section is a card containing a section header followed by at least three labeled toggle rows (a setting name with an on/off switch). The sections all share the same structure and differ only in their data, so factor the repeated markup accordingly rather than copy-pasting it.
- Visual style comes from the app's design tokens: section headers use the muted text color, and each section card sits on a surface background with rounded corners.
- Toggling a switch must flip that setting in the model; every setting is independent.
- Add a status bar showing how many settings are currently enabled out of the total, updating live.
- Keep all logic in src/main.zig (Model, Msg, update); the markup only binds values and dispatches messages.
- Validate the markup with `native markup check src/app.native`, and keep `zig build test` green — extend src/tests.zig so the tests cover toggling at least one setting in each section through the real dispatch path.

When you are done, make sure `zig build test` passes and summarize what you built.

checks.description: markup defines a <template> for the repeated section
markup uses at least one color token style attribute
muted section headers reference the text_muted token
live snapshot shows the three sections and switch widgets

criteria: Model/Msg/update follow The Elm Architecture idiomatically: one message per user intent, no redundant or derivable state stored in the model, update handles every message correctly.
The <template> is well-factored: minimal args that carry data rather than presentation details, no residual copy-paste between sections, sensible names.
Tests are meaningful: they toggle settings through the real dispatch path and assert model state changes (not merely that the code compiles), covering each section.
The markup stays declarative: it only binds model data and dispatches messages; no logic, counting, or formatting is smuggled into the view that belongs in the model.
checks.description: quality of the settings app beyond compliance
